findings.description: The title in the PDF is rendered in a more compact bold block, while the HTML splits it into two separate large heading lines with more spacing between them.
findings.evidence: PDF: 'SUMMARY OF NOTICE OF / INTENT TO REDEVELOP A BROWNFIELDS PROPERTY' appears as a single centered bold block with subheadings. HTML: The title is split into two large heading elements with a horizontal rule separator before the subheadings.

findings.description: The PDF uses justified text alignment for body paragraphs, while the HTML uses left-aligned text.
findings.evidence: PDF body text is fully justified with even right margins. HTML body text is left-aligned with ragged right margins.

findings.description: The URL in the PDF is rendered as a hyperlink with underline styling, while in the HTML it appears as plain text without visible hyperlink formatting.
findings.evidence: PDF: 'https://edocs.deq.nc.gov/WasteManagement/' appears as a blue underlined hyperlink. HTML: same URL appears as plain text.

findings.description: The bold underlined date 'October 1, 2025' in the PDF preserves both bold and underline, while the HTML shows it in bold only without underline.
findings.evidence: PDF: 'October 1, 2025' is bold and underlined. HTML: 'October 1, 2025' appears bold but not underlined.

findings.description: The URL https://edocs.deq.nc.gov/WasteManagement/ is present in the HTML as plain text but not rendered as a clickable hyperlink, whereas in the PDF it appears as a hyperlink. Similarly, the email addresses are plain text in the HTML rather than mailto links. However, no links are fabricated.
findings.evidence: PDF contains hyperlinked URL 'https://edocs.deq.nc.gov/WasteManagement/' and email 'shirley.liggins@deq.nc.gov'. HTML has these as plain text without anchor tags.

findings.description: The HTML contains nested <main> tags, which is a structural duplication of the wrapper element, though the textual content itself is not duplicated.
findings.evidence: HTML has <main> inside <main>, though this is a structural issue rather than content duplication.

findings.description:
The document title is split across two lines in the h1 using a
tag, combining 'SUMMARY OF NOTICE OF' and 'INTENT TO REDEVELOP A BROWNFIELDS PROPERTY'. The PDF also has two additional centered bold lines ('Brownfields Property Name: Colwick Road Property' and 'Brownfields Project Number: 25095-21-060') which could be considered subheadings but are rendered as a paragraph with tags. This is an acceptable representation of the PDF layout.
findings.evidence: PDF has four centered bold header lines; HTML has the first two as h1 and the next two as bold paragraph text.
